concerns.text: The plan proposes training a separate Word2Vec model for each year on 'sequences of genres per track'. This methodology fails to capture temporal evolution because Word2Vec requires a corpus of 'sentences' (sequences) to learn vector space geometry. A single year's list of genres is a bag-of-words, not a sequence. Without a defined sequential structure (e.g., playlist order, listening history), the model cannot learn 'genre proximity' based on co-occurrence in a listening context, rendering the resulting vectors meaningless for measuring 'blurring'.

The sensitivity analysis (FR-006) filters the time-series data to include 'only years where the absolute year-over-year delta in similarity exceeds that threshold'. This introduces severe selection bias (collider bias) into the regression. By conditioning on the outcome (delta), the analysis artificially truncates the variance and biases the slope estimate, making the statistical inference invalid for testing the null hypothesis of no trend.

The plan uses Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) with Robust SE on a time series of N≈20 years. This ignores temporal autocorrelation (AR errors), which is almost certain in genre evolution data. OLS standard errors will be biased downward, inflating Type I error rates (false positives). A time-series appropriate model (e.g., Newey-West, ARIMA, or GLS) is required for valid inference.

The plan defines the input to Word2Vec as 'sequences of genres associated with tracks played in that year'. It is unclear how these sequences are constructed. If tracks are treated as independent documents, Word2Vec reduces to a co-occurrence matrix (equivalent to PMI), not a sequence-based embedding. If playlist order is used, the plan must explicitly state how to handle the massive sparsity and noise in playlist data to ensure the 'sequence' reflects actual listening flow rather than arbitrary playlist ordering.
